page: Vultr voc-s-2c-16gb-320s-amd is a virtual machine instance within the Optimized Cloud Compute family, configured with 2 vCPUs and 16.0 GB of RAM. The underlying hardware features an AMD EPYC processor utilizing the x86_64 architecture, operating at a base clock speed of 2.0 GHz. This configuration allocates 1 physical core with 2 threads per core under a shared CPU allocation model. The system includes 320 GB of local NVMe SSD storage and provides one complimentary public IPv4 address.

Benchmark data reveals a stark contrast between single-core and multi-core or memory-bound performance. The instance achieves top-tier single-core CPU performance in stress-ng testing, alongside strong single-threaded gzip compression and bzip3 decompression speeds. However, its shared CPU allocation and memory architecture introduce performance bottlenecks. Passmark CPU, encryption, and physics scores fall into the lower tier, while database operations, Redis SET operations, and static web serving throughput are weak to poor. Memory bandwidth for small cached blocks is also limited, though large block read and write bandwidth remains average.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, this instance offers a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core and a generous 320 GB NVMe SSD storage allocation at a lower resource tier. This makes it a viable option for memory-intensive but compute-light workloads where continuous dedicated CPU performance is not required. Suitable workloads include lightweight caching layers, development environments, basic file storage, and asynchronous background processing tasks that benefit from larger memory footprints and local NVMe storage without demanding sustained high-throughput database operations.
description: Vultr voc-s-2c-16gb-320s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute instance featuring 2 vCPUs and 16.0 GB of RAM, powered by an AMD EPYC x86_64 processor running at 2.0 GHz. It utilizes a shared CPU allocation with 1 physical core and 2 threads, bundled with 320 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. Performance benchmarks show top-tier single-core CPU execution and strong single-threaded compression capabilities, contrasted by weaker multi-core, database, and static web serving metrics due to its shared resource model. This profile offers qualitative cost efficiency for memory-heavy, compute-light workloads. It is best suited for development environments, lightweight caching, and asynchronous background tasks requiring moderate memory and local storage without sustained CPU demands.
